=====================================================
  DSA TECHNIQUES — QUICK REFERENCE
=====================================================

-----------------------------------------------------
1. TWO POINTER — OPPOSITE ENDS (Converging Pointers)
-----------------------------------------------------
Definition:
  Place one pointer (L) at the start and one pointer (R)
  at the end of the array. Move them toward each other
  based on a condition until they meet.

How it works:
  - If sum < target  → move L rightward (L++)
  - If sum > target  → move R leftward  (R--)
  - If sum == target → found the pair (L++,R--)

Time  Complexity: O(n)
Space Complexity: O(1)

Typical Use Cases:
  - Pair sum in a sorted array
  - Palindrome check
  - Container with most water
  - 3Sum / 4Sum problems

Example:
  Array: [1, 3, 5, 7, 9, 11, 13, 15], Target = 14
  L=0 (val=1), R=7 (val=15) → sum=16 > 14 → R--
  L=0 (val=1), R=6 (val=13) → sum=14 == 14 → Found!

-----------------------------------------------------
2. TWO POINTER — FAST / SLOW (Floyd's Cycle Detection)
-----------------------------------------------------
Definition:
  Two pointers move through a linked list (or array) at
  different speeds. Slow moves 1 step, Fast moves 2 steps.
  If they ever meet, a cycle exists.

How it works:
  - slow = slow.next       (1 step at a time)
  - fast = fast.next.next  (2 steps at a time)
  - If fast == slow → cycle detected
  - If fast reaches null  → no cycle

Time  Complexity: O(n)
Space Complexity: O(1)

Typical Use Cases:
  - Detect cycle in a linked list
  - Find middle of a linked list
  - Find start of a cycle
  - Happy number problem

Example:
  List: 1 → 2 → 3 → 4 → 5 → 3 (cycle back to node 3)
  Step 1: slow=2, fast=3
  Step 2: slow=3, fast=5
  Step 3: slow=4, fast=4  → CYCLE DETECTED!

-----------------------------------------------------
3. SLIDING WINDOW
-----------------------------------------------------
Definition:
  Both pointers (L and R) start at the left. R expands the
  window by moving right. When the window violates a
  constraint, L shrinks it from the left.

How it works:
  - Expand: R++ (add arr[R] to window)
  - Shrink:  L++ (remove arr[L] from window) when constraint breaks
  - Track the best valid window seen so far

Time  Complexity: O(n)
Space Complexity: O(1) to O(k) depending on problem

Typical Use Cases:
  - Maximum sum subarray of size k
  - Longest substring without repeating characters
  - Minimum window substring
  - Subarray with sum <= k

Example:
  Array: [2, 1, 5, 2, 3, 2, 4, 1], k=3 (fixed window)
  Window [5, 2, 3] → sum=10 → max so far

-----------------------------------------------------
4. PREFIX SUM
-----------------------------------------------------
Definition:
  Precompute a cumulative sum array so that any range
  sum query can be answered in O(1) instead of O(n).

How it works:
  - Build: prefix[i] = prefix[i-1] + arr[i]
  - Query: sum(L, R) = prefix[R] - prefix[L-1]

Time  Complexity: O(n) build, O(1) per query
Space Complexity: O(n)

Typical Use Cases:
  - Range sum queries
  - Subarray sum equals k
  - Count subarrays with given XOR
  - 2D matrix range sum

Example:
  Array:  [3,  1,  4,  1,  5,  9,  2]
  Prefix: [3,  4,  8,  9, 14, 23, 25]
  Sum of index 2 to 4 = prefix[4] - prefix[1] = 14 - 4 = 10

-----------------------------------------------------
5. KADANE'S ALGORITHM (Maximum Subarray Sum)
-----------------------------------------------------
Definition:
  At each index, decide whether to extend the current
  subarray or start fresh. Track the global maximum.

How it works:
  - cur  = max(arr[i], cur + arr[i])
  - best = max(best, cur)

Time  Complexity: O(n)
Space Complexity: O(1)

Typical Use Cases:
  - Maximum subarray sum (classic)
  - Maximum product subarray (variant)
  - Circular subarray maximum sum (variant)

Example:
  Array: [-2, 1, -3, 4, -1, 2, 1, -5, 4]
  Max subarray: [4, -1, 2, 1] → sum = 6

=====================================================
  COMPARISON TABLE
=====================================================

Technique          | Time   | Space | Key Idea
-------------------|--------|-------|---------------------------
Opposite Ends      | O(n)   | O(1)  | L & R converge inward
Fast / Slow        | O(n)   | O(1)  | Different speed pointers
Sliding Window     | O(n)   | O(1)  | Expand R, shrink L
Prefix Sum         | O(n)*  | O(n)  | Precompute range sums
Kadane's           | O(n)   | O(1)  | Extend or restart subarray

* O(1) per query after O(n) build

=====================================================
